sentences.text: So the plan ripened to escape.
And we make use of the address that Eddie, Dottie's father, had left behind with, with her parents, namely a smuggler who lived in the town of Tilburg, in southeastern Holland.
He had taken-- he had a so-called organization of smugglers that took young people from Holland through Belgium, Paris, occupied and non-occupied France, to Lyon, up to Lyon.
And in Lyon, we had heard already rumors that there was a Dutch representation.
And they helped those people, those refugees, to get into Switzerland.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We started corresponding and informing.
I informed Dottie that we thought we would not be able to stay for the duration of the war in, in that attic.
First of all, we, we felt that we couldn't, we couldn't stand it.
And secondly, the danger was too high.
All that was not written like I'm telling it now.
It was written in very carefully-chosen words that, that she understood, but anyone who would read it would, would, would think this is gibberish, and what does it mean?
These guys don't even speak a normal language.
But we understood each other.
We had learned to write this kind of secret language.
She agreed.
She also felt that she could not stay for any length of time because for other reasons, one of the reasons being that the two boys went to school.
The wife went-- left the house in the morning to search for food, getting on her bicycle and traveling sometimes 50 miles to, to get food.
And Dottie was there.
And sometimes, he would stay home from business and made advances to her, which she was lucky to fight off.
But that may have been a reason why she willingly and gladly agreed to the plan to try to escape.
It was a very, very dangerous enterprise.
Because we had to travel more than 1,000 miles, crossing four borders.
And all borders were very heavily guarded by that time.
Unlike 1941 when we tried it, now we were in almost a year later.
And things had changed.
So we decided on a date-- the 27th of September, 1942.
Our friend, Lex, was the in-between man.

He started contacting that same smuggler in Tilburg, met with him, arranged everything, arranged the amount of money we had to pay-- half to him, half to the other smuggler in Paris, that we would go via Paris-- first via Belgium, then Paris.
And he got 50% in advance.
He would see to it that we would get false Belgian identity cards without the J, of course, with a Gentile Belgian name, Flemish name, which was very similar to the Dutch names.
And he would wait on the 27th, which was a Sunday, in Tilburg, with three bicycles.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The day of our departure, I went out for the first time from my hiding place without, of course, the yellow star, obviously.
Went to my parents.
My parents in the meantime had also been forced to seek asylum in that same apartment where Dottie was.
And I went there and met Dottie, met my parents, said goodbye to my parents.
And Rudolf-- Rudolf Fuyts that was the name of the man where Dottie was hiding out in his house-- he, Dottie, and I left.
She went first again to a place where she could meet her parents and said goodbye to her parents.
That was the last time she saw them.
And it was the last time I saw my father.
Rudolf Fuyts took us to the central station by, by tram, a street car.
And Max went with Mr.
Wellensiek, also to the central station.
They purchased the tickets for us, the train tickets to Tilburg.
We casually met in, in a compartment of the train.
And we left for, for the Tilburg, for Tilburg.
There, we saw a man standing with three bicycles.
He, he recognized us because he had received our photos, passport photos, from Mr.
Wellensiek to prepare the false Belgium identity cards.
So he recognized us.
We went over.
And the four of us, we pedaled maybe for 15 or 20 minutes direction Belgian border to a farm, an isolated farm.
There, we stepped-- we, we stopped.
And he greeted those people whom he probably knew.
And they, in all likelihood, cooperated with him.
And he told us that a young Belgian guide would come for us after dark to take us in sideways-- in back, back, back ways over the Belgian border to the first Belgian village of [INAUDIBLE].
He gave us instructions and said, he will take you to a place called Menin, or Menen, which is on the French border.
There you will stay overnight.
Then another one will come.
This young guy, this young Belgian guy, will then take you the next day to Paris.
And in Paris, you will stay for three or four days.
Then another one of my partners is going to pick you up after three days and take you to Lyon.
And from there, you are on your own.
And when you are in Lyon with this partner of mine, then you pay him the other half of the money.
Fine.
He left.
The young guide, Belgian guide, came.
He was on a bicycle.
We were walking.
We walked for maybe half an hour, 45 minutes, through fields of potatoes and wheat and all kind, never, never a, a, a dirt road.
We avoided all roads, until he finally stopped.
And we, we could hear music.
He, he guided us to a shack and said, enter here, and wait until I come back for you.
Must have been 11:30 at night.
Curfew was at midnight.
In the shack of middle of nowhere, but it must have been in the neighborhood of, of a restaurant or something.
Because we heard the music quite clearly.
It must, it must have belonged to that restaurant.
After half an hour, he came.
It had been quiet down.
Everything was quiet.
It was after midnight.
And he took us to that restaurant.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We spoke-- with the owner.
He came and brought us some, some blankets.
And we laid down.
He said, I will call you tomorrow early.
I come back tomorrow early at 7:00 to pick you up.
He will give you breakfast.
And then we are on our way.
That, it, it happened just like that.
We took a streetcar to Antwerp, then the train to Brussels.
And from Brussels, we stepped over to another train to the border to Menen, or Menin, as it is called in, in Flemish.
We went through the street of Menin.
And we noticed a lot of black shirts.
Those were the Belgian fascists.
And he took us to a little hotel and said, everything is prepaid here.
You stay here overnight.
Tomorrow morning at 8:00, I'll be here to take you to Paris.
We stayed there.
He never came.
And by 9:00 next morning, we knew he would never come back.
People started asking question.
They heard us speak Dutch instead of Flemish.
Flemish is a dialect.
Yeah, are you from Holland?
Yeah.
We are on our way to Paris.
We are waiting for our guide.
So it became too, too risky.
We left.
But where to?
We didn't know.
We didn't know that place.
So we walked and walked and walked until we reached the Main Street.
Did you have any maps?
We had no map.
But we noticed this was the-- probably the most important street, because it was crowded and, and men and women, and many German military men.
We saw, we saw SS.
And we saw black shirts walking along.
And finally we said, where are we going?
Finally, we saw a cafe.
And we entered that cafe.
There was a woman alone.
I started talking to the woman.
And after a few words, I noticed she was of the what we call the good kind, not, not Nazi.
And I told her what has happened to us.
She said, I wish I could help you.
But I can't do anything for you.
Then I remembered, or, or Dottie reminded me, of a postcard that Eddie had sent to his parents from Switzerland.
He had, he had mentioned the place, Menen.
And he said, in Menen, I, I met this good friend, George, you know, the one with a lot of jewelry, jewelry-- arm and watches and rings.
But he's such a nice guy.
So I said to the woman, do you know anyone by the name of George?
I don't know whether that is a first name.
I don't whether it's a last name.
But he, he is well-dressed and, and wears a lot of jewelry.
She said, no.
I don't.
But I tell you.
If you walk two blocks up the Main Street, there is a restaurant.
Don't take the first one.
That is a hangout for the Belgian fascists.
The second one, if you go there, the owner knows practically everyone here in town.
Try to speak to him.
He's, he's a good guy.
So I left.
And I said to, to Dottie and Max, you must stay here until I come back.
And if something happens to me, you two must try to continue someplace, to go back.
Do whatever you think is the best way.
I went to, went to that restaurant, passed the first one, and saw that it was crowded with black shirts and, and Hakenkreuz, the swastika, and Germans.
I passed it and went to the second one, sat down, took a glass of beer.
The owner came.
And I started talking to me.
And I explained to him, I'm coming from Paris.
I have a message for a-- someone here in town.
I lost that address.
I don't know any more.
All I know is his name is George.
And he wears a lot of jewelry.
Oh, you mean George [?
Kemperland. ?] Sure, I know him.
I said, can you give me his address?
Sure-- wrote down the address, told me how to get there.
I rushed over.
His wife opened and-- the door.
I explained to her, I'm Eddie [?
Parfumer's ?] brother-in-law.
Oh, I remember Eddie [?
Parfumer. ?] Come in.
My husband isn't here.
Wait.
He will be here any minute.
But that any minute took about half an hour.
And I was dying through all those 30 minutes, worrying what happens to Dottie and Max.
Will they wait?
Or what is going on?
Finally, [?
Kemperland ?] came.
In a few hurried words, I told him.
He immediately knew who [?
Parfumer-- ?] Eddie [?
Parfumer ?] was.
I told him what has happened to us.
He said, I can help you.
I get you to Paris.
I get you to Lyon.
I said, look, we have lost a lot of money now.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] How much will we have to pay you?
He started with a high amount.
I said, we can't pay you that.
I don't have the money.
So finally, we agreed on an amount.
He says, you pay me half.
The other half, you pay to my purser when he reaches Lyon with you-- same story.
I hurried back.
I came to the cafe.
I saw only Dottie.
My heart just sank.
I said, where is Max?
He left to look for you.
I said, damn it!
What is he looking?
Where does he look?
If I had been arrested, the only thing that could have happened to him is also to be arrested.
Thank God, finally, he came back.
I cursed him left and right.
I said you-- I said to you, don't leave this place.
And don't leave Dottie alone.
Excuse me.
Now, we rush to, to George [?
Kemperland. ?] He took us to a-- he said, listen.
When we-- the, the border runs through Menen, the French border, similar to what the-- what we had before, but this time, really, through a town.
Menen was a town of maybe 20,000 people.
I know that, that French border guard.
Let me go first.
I will call you.
And you'll follow.
Give me your Belgian identity cards.
We gave him the identity cards.
He went there, whispered with this guy.
We passed through.
And we were now in France, occupied France.
We walked for maybe another five, 10 minutes, came to a, a small hotel.
He was greeted very heartily by his, by his friends whom he had there.
And we stayed there overnight.
Next morning, George and a friend of his came to pick us up.
And we took an extremely crowded train loaded with workmen from-- to [?
Villa de Eppe ?] to-- not [?
Villa de Eppe, ?] [?
Villa de L'ile, ?] via [INAUDIBLE] to Paris.
In Paris, we left the train station.
First, he walked first, then, then Dottie, then Max.
And I was the last one.
And I had a briefcase in my hand, just like Mark.
And Dottie had, I think, a little hand bag or whatever, in her hand.
As we were walking out towards the exit, someone tapped me on my shoulder.
I swirled around.
And I saw a French gendarme with his hat.
I thought, this is it.
He said to me in French, open this, this handbag-- this briefcase of yours.
I opened it.
There were clothing in it, nothing else.
Oh, you have no food.
OK.
Pardon.
And I could leave.
He took-- George took us to Montmartre.
That is the red light district of Paris, to a bordello.
And, and he knew the woman, the madame.
And the woman told us we should go up, took us to the third floor, and gave us a room there in her own, her own living quarters, not the business part.
And George said, I'm leaving now.
You will have to wait three days until Francois comes back.
And he is the guy who will take you to Lyon.
That happened.
He came.
We didn't have a very good impression of that man.
We didn't like him from the first moment on.
But we had no choice.
He instructed us to, to come the next morning to the Gare de Lyon, one of the railroad stations of Paris, early in the morning.
And we did that.
He was there.
We went to the train.
He said, look.
I'll sit in the same compartment with you.
But you are not to talk to me if people are around.
You speak with an accent.
And everybody will notice that you are not French.
So don't ignore me unless I talk to you.
Fine.
We left on our way to Dijon.
And we were about an hour out of Paris when he called me to come outside.
Outside, he said, now you have to pay me the rest of the money.
I said, wait a minute.
George told me that I have to pay you the money when we are in Lyon.
He said, I don't care what, what George told you.
I'm telling you that you have to pay me the money now.
You don't pay me, I get off in Dijon.
And you are on your own.
Went back to Dottie and Mark.
Our compartment was empty.
We were the only four people there.
And, and, they, they agreed with me that we had no choice but to pay him.
So we paid him.
We came to Dijon, waited for a train to Le Croisic.
Le Croisic is a very famous French town for coal mining and iron works and ammunition factories.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when we got, he said to us, when we get to Le Croisic, we have to wait for a bus that comes every half hour and takes us to Montceau-les-Mines.
Montceau-les-Mines is very close to the, to the demarcation line.
Demarcation line was the border between occupied France and non-occupied France.
We stayed there overnight.
And then the next morning, I take you over the demarcation line.
And from there, we take a train to Lyon.
Fine.
We sat down in a, in a cafe and waited and waited and waited.
It must have been two hour, three hours, four hours.
We don't know how long.
The bus didn't come.
There were far more people standing at the bus stop.
And he went over a few times.
And I said, what happens?
He said, the bus is not coming.
The bus, we don't know what happened.
The bus is not coming.
Finally, finally, it was, it was late in the afternoon.
The bus came.
Now he said, listen, come over.
You will walk all the way to the back of the bus.
And I'm going to sit in the front.
We don't know each other.
When I get off, I will wait until you, you catch up with me.
And then I take you to the, to the pub, or to the whatever, the beer hall, where we will-- where you will stay overnight.
OK.
The bus left.
It was dark by now.
Must have been 9:00 at night, 10:00 at night, perhaps, and old, dilapidated bus.
We were, we were riding maybe half hour when the bus suddenly stopped in the middle of nowhere.
It was dark.
Everything was dark.
And they started talking.
And I heard something of bombardment-- bombing.
Didn't know what, what it was.
People got out.
The driver got out with his-- a torch-- with a flashlight and came back in again.
People started talking.
Then I heard a neighbor of mine say, the English had tried to bomb Le Croisic at night, had bombed the main street.
And the bus couldn't continue.
The street was totally destroyed.
So we turned around, went back to where we had come from, and went a roundabout way.
In short, it was about 11:00 at night when we arrived at the bus terminal in Montceau-les-Mines.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As we heard later form the owner of that pub, he said, what that guy wanted you to do is sit in the back because each bus that comes from Le Croisic, usually two SS guys are standing at the terminal and check the paper of each one that comes out.
That's why he wanted you to sit in the back.
You know what he wanted to do?
He wanted you to be arrested by these SS.
Because you had paid him the money?
Yes, you did.
So that's why.
He wanted you to be killed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was his purpose.
But because of the late hour and the, the destroyed road, the Germans must have thought there is no bus coming anymore.
And there was no checking of papers.
When we got off, we could see that guy at the corner.
We rushed after him.
He led us through dark streets.
Then he said, wait here a minute.
I have to see that everything is, is, is OK, that's no bad people are there.
He went through the back door, left through the front door.
After a few minutes, we noticed he has abandoned us.
We went inside.
And as you heard probably from Dottie, then the woman didn't want us to stay there.
She was afraid that she might, might be arrested.
I pleaded with her.
I pleaded with her.
And while I was pleading with her, the door opened and two German border guards-- men in their '40s, perhaps-- walked in with their, with their--
With their guns.
And the woman, the Frenchwoman, saved our life, our lives, By having the presence of mind to rush and give them beer and beer.
And she drowned them in beer.
And so he forgot to ask our papers.

At one moment he-- at a certain moment when he was half drunk already, both were half drunk, he-- the, the, sergeant, the corporal, rather, he spoke a little bit of French, he asked the French woman, who are these guys?
And she said with a noncommittal question, [FRENCH SPEECH].
They are travelers, which meant nothing.
At that-- he didn't, he didn't pursue the matter.
These few words saved our lives.
Because she could have easily have said, I don't know them.
They just came.
And that would have been our end.
But she didn't.
With this very, very intelligent and, and, and, and, and wonderful way, she saved us by, by being noncommittal.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We met at-- one of the, the people present was a coal miner.
After the Germans had left, he said, let's see whether I can help you.
Maybe my cousin will be able to, to take you over the border.
He disappeared.
We waited about 15 minutes.
He came back, said, he's willing to do it.
By that time, it was already perhaps 12:30, 1:00 at night.
